data.title: Inflation Expectations Vs. 10-Year Treasury Yield - The (2nd) Great Conundrum
data.description:
The first Great Conundrum was in the early 2000s after the 2001 – 2002 50% bear market in the S&P 500 and the start of Gulf War II in March, 2003. Alan Greenspan started hiking the fed funds rate (if memory serves correctly, in 2004–2005 (?), but the Treasury yield curve started flattening out.

Rick Santelli, the great CNBC reporter would do live shots from the 30-year Treasury pit at the then-CBOT (Chicago Board of Trade), which is now part of the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) and Rick kept citing the “+90% levels of bearishness” on the part of Treasury futures traders, and yet the long-end of the Treasury curve kept rallying in spite of all this.

I don’t know if Greenspan called it the “great conundrum” or just “the conundrum”, but what it was foreshadowing (ultimately) was 2007 and 2008, and the worst recession since the 1930s.

To put that period into context, the 2001–2002 bear market for the S&P 500 was only the 2nd 50% correction in the post WW II period, and the first 50% correction since 1973 – 1974. Coming out of that in the early to mid-2000’s, the Street expected a roaring economy under a Republican President (Bush 43) particularly given the fiscal stimulus from the Gulf War, and rising inflation, (hence the bond bearishness), and it just never happened.

The 10-year Treasury yield rose from 3.5% in 2003 to 5% in 2007, but the move in early 1987 was much, much faster, and much more damaging.

The (2nd) conundrum:

The first chart above is from Lisa Abramowicz, who is a Bloomberg reporter and does a good job at it, and the graph is from late March ’25, and shows the rising inflation expectations “pre-Liberation Day” with the 2nd chart being from Liz Ann Sonders of Schwab, from an April 29th post on X, also showing rising inflation expectations.

Every economic and money and banking text educated me on how “inflation expectations” were always more critical to bond-market participants than actual inflation, which makes sense since “expectations” are forward-looking, but there is a definite disconnect today since the 10-year Treasury yield is down from 4.80% in January ’25, to 4.30% today.

Do you believe “price” or the economic data?

What’s the disconnect telling us today? (Maybe nothing. Maybe it’s just how inflation expectations are measured.)

Summary/conclusion

For a little fun and to use the historical comparison, the disconnect between inflation expectations and the 10-year Treasury yield isn’t being labeled a new conundrum yet, but it’s puzzling for sure.

In today’s FOMC statement, I truly wonder if Jay Powell will allude to the fact that if the President hadn’t set off the “tariff kerfuffle” as it’s been described, Jay Powell and the FOMC may have had adequate reason to start lowering the fed funds already, given the very good inflation data from the core CPI and the Core CPE from January to March ’25, and the falling price of crude oil.

The Fed Chair is now forced to wait and see how the tariff policy changes impact pricing and thus inflation through the 2nd quarter and even into the 3rd quarter.

None of this is advice or a recommendation but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. None of this information may be updated and if updated may not be done in a timely fashion.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Uber's Q1 Earnings Reveal What The Market Still Doesn't Get
data.description:
Introduction

Uber Technologies, Inc.’s (NYSE:UBER) Q1 2025 report triggered a sharp reaction in the market, with the stock falling over 4% in pre-market trading. But if we zoom out from the slight revenue miss and look at what really matters – profitability, user growth, cash generation, and AV traction – this quarter was actually a powerful confirmation of everything I laid out in my previous article, “Uber Stock: Why Driverless Cars and Tariffs Are Actually Tailwinds.”

The market may not have liked what it saw, but I did. And anyway, the market has been reacting strangely lately.

Uber’s Results: What Happened This Quarter?

Uber posted revenue of $11.53 billion, falling a little short of the $11.62 billion consensus. But that’s where the bad news ends. Everything else was better than expected. Earnings per share came in at $0.83, beating expectations by over 60%. Adjusted EBITDA hit a record $1.9 billion (up 35% year-over-year), and free cash flow exceeded $2.3 billion. Uber also turned net cash positive for the first time in a while, moving from negative $1.1 billion a year ago to $401 million in positive net cash.

So why did the stock drop? Firstly, because the market is volatile, people are nervous, and any piece of news that is bad, and even sometimes good, sends the stocks down. Second, for some reason, Uber is often compared to Tesla (TSLA), and if one goes up, the other goes down. Tesla, not long ago, missed on everything, but the stock soared. What would be more logical than falling Uber stock after beating almost all the expectations?

Besides, I liked the tone of the management from the call – it was confident but grounded, which is perfect under the current macro conditions. CEO Dara Khosrowshahi emphasized that the company isn’t satisfied with “good,” they’re aiming for “great,” citing a packed quarter of launches, partnerships, and record margins. There was no defensiveness around the revenue miss; instead, the team pointed to continued trip growth, high user engagement, and robust international expansion. CFO Prashant Mahendra-Rajah was clear about the company’s priorities: driving sustainable margin expansion without sacrificing growth investments in AV, delivery, and global markets.

It was the tone of a team that knows where it’s going, and it reassured me, as an investor in this company.

Among others, great beats to pay attention to:

  • Uber generated $2.3 billion in free cash flow, up from $549 million a year ago.

  • They swung to a positive net cash position of $401 million.

  • Monthly active users hit 170 million, up 14% YoY.

  • Trips grew 18%, with engagement at all-time highs globally.

  • Advertising and cost discipline boosted margins across both Delivery and Mobility.

And yet... because revenue was missed by $90 million, the market pulled back. What can I say, for long-term investors, that’s an opportunity, and seeing how the stock jumps higher while I am writing this, many of you are already working on catching this “dip.”

Segment Performance

Mobility continues to power Uber’s cash engine. Trip growth held steady at 18% YoY, with particularly strong traction in international markets and less dense U.S. cities. In fact, sparse markets now account for 20% of total trips and are growing faster than urban centers. That’s great because, in these markets, users are more willing to pay for services like Uber Reserve, where reliability matters more than price, creating higher-margin opportunities that wouldn’t scale as easily in dense, competitive urban zones. In fact, during the call, Dara said:

In some of these markets, people kind of use reserve in order to drive reliability in the suburbs. About 40% of reserve trips are now not related to travel as well, so it’s becoming kind of a everyday habit going out to dinner. And I do think that the concentration of reserve in these less dense markets is gonna be higher than in urban markets as people are willing to pay a premium for higher reliability in these less dense markets.

Surprisingly enough, delivery is becoming a profit driver for Uber. This quarter, the segment earned a margin of 3.7% on gross bookings (an improvement of 70 basis points from last year). That might sound small, but in a business at Uber’s scale, it’s a big deal. The improvement is mostly thanks to advertising revenue and the efficiencies that come from operating at such a large size. On top of that, Uber confirmed that its grocery and retail delivery services, which only just broke even last quarter, are now actually contributing to profits. Even more impressive is that the delivery segment brought a 9% incremental margin this quarter. That shows just how profitable this part of the business can become as it grows.

Autonomous Vehicles

In my last article, I emphasized that Uber’s strategy around AV wasn’t priced in, and this quarter only strengthens that view. In Austin, Uber’s fleet of Waymo-powered AVs is now outperforming 99% of human drivers in terms of trip volume per vehicle. Besides, Uber is now scaling these partnerships, with five announced across the U.S., Europe, and the Middle East. High utilization means the economics could work, and Uber’s platform gives them a natural advantage in integrating these vehicles seamlessly.

Uber Technologies (NYSE:UBER) and WeRide (WRD) announced on Monday a significant expansion of their previously announced strategic partnership, adding 15 additional cities globally over the next five years, including in Europe.

Guidance & Outlook

Management guided Q2 EPS to $0.79 (above consensus) and reiterated their focus on balancing margin growth with investment in expansion – AV, sparse markets, and international delivery. They also expect insurance headwinds to ease meaningfully this year, thanks to improved driver behavior monitoring, litigation reform, and more efficient pricing.

CEO Dara Khosrowshahi summed it up best:

Good is not going to be good enough. We need to be great.

Conclusion

This quarter, for me at least, was a reconfirmation of Uber’s transition into a high-margin, high-cash-flow global platform. The revenue miss spooked traders, but the fundamentals look stronger than ever.

I reaffirm my Strong Buy rating. In fact, I believe this dip presents a buying opportunity. Uber is executing in everything – operational leverage, user growth, international expansion, AV integration, and delivery monetization. I still believe the market is underestimating just how scalable and sticky this business has become and where it can grow in the future.

data.title: Sarepta: ELEVIDYS DMD Treatment Setback Brings Downgrade To Hold Rating
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:SRPT) it was in an article entitled "Sarepta Therapeutics: BLA Filing of SRP-9003 For LGMD Is The Next Step." In this article, I noted that the company was continuing to develop its gene therapy SRP-9003 for the treatment of patients with limb-girdle muscular dystrophy [LGMD]. Specifically, the company is pursuing LGMD patients who are of the subtype 2E/R4 or beta-sarcoglycanopathy. Well, considerable progress has been made as it met with the FDA, and it was revealed that it was able to file under an Accelerated Approval pathway for this program.

With that being said, should this program continue to progress well, then it will be able to submit its Biologics License Application [BLA] of SRP-9003 as a gene therapy for the treatment of these patients with 2E/R4 LGMD to the FDA in the 2nd half of 2025. I believe that this specific program of the development of SRP-9003 opens the door for further shareholder value creation. Despite this advancement, I'm changing the rating of this stock from a "Strong Buy" to a "Hold Rating." My reasoning for this is because of the uncertainty surrounding sales of its new Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy [DMD] drug ELEVIDYS. It was noted that this gene therapy brought in sales of $375 million into the company's coffers. Not bad at all, but the problem is that it was a considerable miss, below 11% of analysts' expectations for starters.

Plus, what also didn't help was the fact that there was a report of one patient death who took this gene therapy to treat their DMD disease as well. In light of that, this may have pressed some patients to pause taking this treatment for their disease. In essence, Sarepta is awaiting an autopsy report of the patient's death, and that will be key to determining what exactly happened.

If the FDA for whatever reason pulls ELEVIDYS off the market, then that would be a huge blow to the company and its stock. That is when I would probably change the rating from a Hold to a Sell. However, for now, the gene therapy is still being marketed. Lower guidance occurred because of the lower-than-expected sales of ELEVIDYS, but things could quickly turn around if the setback around the single patient death being resolved.

Risk-Benefit Profile Of ELEVIDYS Remains In Limbo

Starting off with the bad news, Sarepta Therapeutics had a few setbacks with respect to sales of its gene therapy ELEVIDYS, which was developed for the treatment of patients with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy [DMD]. The first setback occurred back in March 2025, when it was announced that there was one patient who had died after having received the company's adeno-associated viruses [AAV] gene therapy to treat their disease. At that time, it was said that the patient suffered from acute liver failure [ALF]. What was in question and still lingering to this day is if the therapy itself caused this patient's death.

Sarepta seems to be inclined to note that despite this therapy being given to more than 800 patients, this was the first report of a patient who developed acute liver failure leading to their death. Why there might be a different outcome here despite this note of a possible liver injury due to the patient being given an AAV gene therapy? That's because it was also noted that this patient had cytomegalovirus [CMV] infection at the same time. Thus, while not entirely guaranteed, this could have also been a contributing factor leading to the patient's death.

As I noted above at the beginning of this article, Sarepta will get the autopsy report in the next few months. It will then become clearer on what exactly was the root cause of the death itself.

However, acute liver failure is no stranger to gene therapies, which have been known to cause this. For instance, Novartis (NVS) ZOLGENSMA is an AAV-based vector gene therapy that has been approved by the FDA for the treatment of pediatric patients less than 2 years of age with Spinal Muscular Atrophy [SMA] with mutation of the SMN1 gene. Under its U.S. prescribing information, with a Box Warning, it notes that Acute Liver failure is a known issue with this AAV-based gene therapy. Thus, at this point for Sarepta there is no way to conclude that ELEVIDYS didn't contribute to the patient's death.

Until the final autopsy report notes otherwise, this issue is a concern for Sarepta going forward. It is important to note that this overhang may have had an impact on sales of ELEVIDYS. In the most recent Q1 of 2025 earnings report from the company, it stated that it generated $375 million in net product revenues of this therapy during this particular quarter. While this might seem ideal, it was 11% lower than what analysts were expecting. Not only that, but this was also a cause to force the company to lower its full-year 2025 revenue guidance going forward. As a matter of fact, it revised its net product revenue guidance to be between $2.3 - $2.6 billion in full-year 2025.

Truthfully, there is uncertainty here because there is no assurance that sales will recover because of the one patient death. On the flip side, if it is found that the patient died due to CMV infection or other possible cause for the acute liver failure, then it might be able to see an immediate recovery in terms of the revenue it could generate going forward.

Potential Fall-Back Option Of SRP-9003 For The Treatment Of Patients With Limb Girdle Muscular Dystrophy

There is one other reason why I have a Hold rating on Sarepta Therapeutics, and that is because of its development of another AAV gene therapy being pursued, known as SRP-9003. This particular candidate is being developed to treat patients with Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy [LGMD]. However, it is important to state that there are several subtypes of this disorder. Not only that, but the company is starting off with a late-stage program thus far with one subtype in particular. This is the ability to use SRP-9003 to go after type 2E/R4 LGMD patients [Those with beta-sarcoglycanopoathies].

It is said that in the 7 major markets, the global limb-girdle muscular dystrophy market is set to reach $865 million by 2035. This is a considerable market and this is another avenue that Sarepta could gain a presence in. However, that is abiding that it attains highly positive biomarker data from the ongoing late-stage study using SRP-9003 to treat these type 2E/R4 LGMD patients for starters. Also, that it is able to ultimately receive Accelerated Approval of this gene therapy to treat this specific group of LGMD patients.

In order for the company to determine if SRP-9003 as an AAV gene therapy will be able to treat these patients with type 2E/R4 LGMD, it is in the process of running the phase 3 SRP-9003-301 [EMERGENE] study. This particular trial is expected to recruit up to a total of 17 patients who are to be given a one-time intravenous [IV] infusion of this gene therapy to treat their disease. It is important to note that this specific late-stage trial is separating patients into two different cohort types, and they are as follows:

  • Cohort 1 - Ambulatory LGMD patients
  • Cohort 2 - Non-Ambulatory LGMD patients.

The thing is that there are many subtypes of LGMD, but in the case of this specific 2E/R4 patients, they are missing beta-sarcogylcan [SGCB] gene. Thus, the point of SRP-9003 is to restore functional SGCB genes to the patient, thus having sufficient muscle contraction and increased skeletal/muscle structure for these patients. With that said, the primary efficacy measure is going to be the change from baseline in beta-sarcoglycan production at day 60 post-dose.

What's even better is that Sarepta had already met with the Office of Therapeutic Products [OTP] in a pre-BLA meeting. With such a meeting, it was said that the company could be eligible under an Accelerated Approval pathway for this particular program. Thus, barring positive beta-sarcoglycan production data from this ongoing phase 3 SRP-9003-301 EMERGENE study, it will be able to submit a BLA of this AAV gene therapy [AAVrh74 vector] to the U.S. FDA in the 2nd half of 2025. The biomarker data itself, from this particular late-stage trial, is set to be released during the 1st half of 2025.

Lastly, the LGMD program itself has expansion opportunities abound. In terms of one gene therapy candidate, SRP-9004, it is being developed to target LGMD patients of the type 2D/R3 in the phase 1 DISCOVERY study. Enrollment and dosing have been completed and hopefully, upon the release of results from this particular study, it will show that patients experience an improvement of alpha-sarcoglycanopathy here.

The other particular gene therapy candidate SRP-9005 is targeting LGMD patients of the type 2C/R5. With the input Sarepta received for this particular therapy candidate, it has been given a green light from the FDA to advance Study SRP-9005-101 [COMPASS] study. The hope with this and the other studies I mentioned above is that the company is able to expand the presence of several gene therapies to treat a wide variety of LGMD patients.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Sarepta Therapeutics had $647.5 million of cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash and investments as of March 31st of 2025. This is broken down into $249 million of cash and cash equivalents, $391 million of investments and lastly $15.6 million of non-current investments. There is definitely a financial risk here, despite the fact that the company is generating revenue from several of its approved PMO drugs being used to treat patients with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy. Along with the approved ELEVIDYS gene therapy to treat this very same patient population.

Why do I claim that there is a financial risk? It is because precisely what it had guided in is 10-Q SEC filing, which is that it believes this cash on hand is sufficient for a cash runway to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months.

With that being said, there is the possibility of it having to dilute shareholders to raise additional funds at some point during this year. While it had to revise revenue guidance for its full-year 2025 to be between $2.3 billion - $2.6 billion, there was a positive that can actually be gleaned from the Q1 of 2025 earnings release. This was the fact that Q1 of 2025 net product revenues were $611.5 million, which ended up being an increase of 70% year-over-year.

The premise is that if it can end up showing that the one patient death wasn't primarily caused by ELEVIDYS itself, then it might be able to see an uptick in the patients treated with this gene therapy. On the other hand, if the autopsy does reveal that this gene therapy was the main cause of the patient's death, then it will be hard to recover in terms of revenue for this AAV gene therapy for the treatment of patients with DMD.

Risks To Business

There are several risks for investors to consider here before investing in Sarepta Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing investigation relating to the autopsy of the one patient death that had occurred with respect to the patient being given ELEVIDYS to treat their DMD disease. There is no assurance that upon completion of the investigation that the company is going to have its drug cleared from being the root cause of the patient's death. Thus, if it is shown that the patient died due to this gene therapy, then it could cause the company's net product revenue to decrease going forward.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing development of SRP-9003 for the treatment of patients with LGMD of type 2E/R4 in the ongoing phase 3 EMERGENE study. The risk here is that there is no assurance that this particular gene therapy is going to be able to produce a sufficient amount of beta-sarcoglycans for these patients to help treat their disease. In addition, it is the goal of Sarepta to be in a position to file a BLA of SRP-9003 for the treatment of these LGMD of type 2E/R4 in the latter part of this year. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the FDA is going to accept the BLA in its present form, nor that it will ultimately be able to receive FDA U.S. marketing approval of SRP-9003 for the treatment of these specific LGMD patients.

The third and final risk to consider here would be the financial position that this company now finds itself in. The reason why is because it achieved a slowing of sales of its DMD gene therapy ELEVIDYS in the most recent quarter. The patient death may have been the root cause of this decline in net product revenues. Plus, also the reason why the company has revised its guidance to be in the lower range of $2.3 - $2.6 billion for full-year 2025 forecast. With that said, it stated in its 10-Q SEC Filing, that it believes it only has enough cash on hand to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months. In essence, this means that it will likely need to raise additional funds in the next several months. If it were to do this, then it would dilute existing shareholders, and this is a major risk going forward.

Conclusion

For the time being, I believe that Sarepta Therapeutics should remain with a Hold rating. The reason why I'm setting it at this particular rating is again because where it goes from here highly depends upon the final autopsy report for the one patient death that occurred with ELEVIDYS. Not only that, but there are two ways where it might be able to generate further shareholder value. One way would be in terms of the advancement of the multiple LGMDs gene therapy candidates being advanced, like: SRP-9002, SRP-9004 and SRP-9005. Above and beyond this particular program advancement, I believe that there might be a shot with an earlier-stage one that is being advanced.

Sarepta was able to establish a collaboration agreement with Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR) in order to advance small interfering RNA [siRNA] candidates to be developed to target patients with Myotonic Dystrophy Type 1 [DM1] and Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy [FSHD]. Speaking of which, Sarepta intends to release data from both of these targeted indications later this year. With positive data readouts here, these might be other avenues that could be in place to continue to generate shareholder value.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Collapse Of Tourism: A Hidden Cost Of The Ongoing Trade War
data.description:
The trade war is making us focus on what the impact may be in terms of exports and imports for various countries, but we are missing some factors that could indirectly make a difference to US economic growth: I believe that the tourism industry plays a more important role than we might think.

The US is the third most visited country in the world and this industry accounts for 2.50% of GDP. But it is not just a question of revenue, it is also about jobs. This industry provides 18 million jobs in the country, roughly 1 in 10 workers, 6 million more than the manufacturing industry. It is essential that the US tourism industry remains healthy, but a problem has arisen in the last two months: there is a sharp decline in international tourism.

The worrying thing is that the deterioration of this industry was completely unexpected in recent months. Adam Sacks, president of Tourism Economics, predicted a 9% increase in international tourism to the US in 2025 at the end of 2024, but today this estimate has been revised significantly downward to -12%. From almost double-digit growth to a double-digit decline in just a few months.

What are the potential impacts on GDP and why is this happening?

What triggered the deterioration of the tourism industry?

There is no point beating around the bush: it is crystal clear that the elephant in the room is the current trade war that began with the introduction of tariffs. Clearly, the problem does not lie in the attractiveness of US cities as vacation destinations, but in more complex political issues. President Trump himself believes that this is the problem: “There's a little nationalism there, I guess, perhaps.”

While it is true that, apart from China, other countries have been more reluctant to retaliate against the tariffs, foreign citizens are doing exactly the opposite. In addition to several cases of boycotts of US products, it seems that international tourists are choosing other destinations over the United States “for patriotic reasons.” Citizens affected by US tariffs have taken the new administration's change in international policy as a personal affront and are trying to make a difference in their own small way. In addition to patriotism, there is another aspect that frankly surprised me: international tourists seem concerned about traveling to the United States because it is considered a 'hostile state'.

Am I going to be detained at the border? What documents do I need? Those are the things that are really stopping the majority of travelers from coming.

Lena Ross — chair of the board of IITA and chief operating officer at America 4 You

The New York Times reports that German tourists have been detained for weeks and then expelled from the United States; Canadian Jasmine Mooney has also experienced similar treatment. Clearly, these reports are causing panic among travelers and are not helping the country's image. Personally, I am surprised by all this, as I was in New York just last month and did not encounter any hostility. However, this is my personal opinion, and clearly not everyone has had the same experiences or feelings as me.

The problem is that European governments themselves are issuing new warnings for those planning to travel to the United States. Finland and Denmark are doing the same, referring in particular to complications that transgender people may face when entering the United States.

The atmosphere is far from relaxed, and tourists no longer seem comfortable spending their vacations in the United States. The image above shows that Germany's Federal Foreign Office has stated that even Germans with a valid permit to enter the United States could be turned away and have no possibility of legal recourse. Putting myself in the shoes of a German tourist, faced with this possibility, I would definitely consider other destinations for my vacation.

Pedro Rios, the director of the American Friends Service Committee, has been helping migrants for 22 years and has never seen such regularity in the detention of Canadians and Europeans:

It's definitely unusual with these cases so close together, and the rationale for detaining these people doesn't make sense. The only reason I see is there is a much more fervent anti-immigrant atmosphere.

Clearly, vacationing in the United States is not a requirement, so such an atmosphere can only drive tourists away. In the next paragraph we will look at the numbers behind what is happening, and I think you will be surprised.

The worrying figures for the tourism industry

European interest in traveling to the United States has fallen dramatically, as we can see from a sharp decline in recent months in almost all major countries:

We are talking about double-digit declines, reaching 30% in some cases. It should be noted that the collapse could be influenced by a technical factor, namely that Easter was in March last year. However, this calendar variation alone cannot explain such a lack of interest in the United States.

The situation is no better for Canadians, with car trips to the US falling by 32% in March. The trade war is still ongoing, and I don't see how this trend can be reversed. According to a study by Longwoods International, 60% of Canadians said that the new administration's current international policies make them less likely to visit the US; 36% had planned to visit the US but changed their minds.

All this will necessarily have a significant impact on the tourism sector, which includes not only hotels and restaurants, but also airlines, transport companies, and small local businesses. The phenomenon is quite serious and, in my opinion, not talked about enough. Here are some figures to give you an idea of what we are talking about.

Canadians are the leading international tourists in the US. In 2024, there were 20.40 million visits, with total spending amounting to $20.50 billion, supporting 140,000 American jobs. At this rate, there will be a 30% drop in Canadian tourist arrivals in 2025, meaning a loss of $6.15 billion, as well as the potential loss of tens of thousands of jobs. And that's just Canadian tourists.

According to estimates by J.P. Morgan, foreign tourist spending in 2024 accounted for 0.70% of US GDP. Assuming a general collapse in tourist spending of 30%, this alone would negatively impact GDP in 2025 by approximately -0.20%. However, this estimate does not take into account everything that revolves around the tourism industry. If there are fewer tourists, there will be less need for labor, and therefore there will be layoffs. An increase in layoffs weighs negatively on consumption, triggering a vicious cycle that can go well beyond -0.20%.

Bottom line, it doesn't bode well for the US tourism industry, potentially another victim of tariffs.

data.title: Ametek Acquires Robotics Assets With FARO Technologies
data.description:
Ametek, Inc. (NYSE:AME) is in a position of strength to navigate this highly uncertain market as U.S. tariff policies become more solidified over the course of the next quarter. Despite the market uncertainty, AME’s well-diversified manufacturing footprint and global sales presence will allow the firm to sell regionally and shift supply chains in order to mitigate most of the risks of tariff policy.

Subsequent to Q1'25, AME announced the acquisition of FARO Technologies, Inc. (NASDAQ:FARO) in a deal valued at $920mm. From a valuation perspective, the acquisition is accretive. Operationally, the acquisition should be minimally dilutive to operating margins given the size comparison between the two firms.

Given the risks and uncertainties in the market, I am recommending AME with a HOLD rating with a price target of $172/share at 16.91x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Looking out beyond the near-term market headwinds, I believe shares should be priced at $188/share at 18.40x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Ametek Operations

Ametek reported a relatively flat quarter in terms of revenue in Q1’25 with an 8% increase in orders when compared to the previous year’s quarter. Despite the flat quarter, the organization increased its operating margin by 55bps to 26.26%.

Electromechanical [EMG] revenue grew by 2% in Q1’25 when compared to Q1’24, offset by a -1% decline in electronic instruments (EIG) sales. Margin improvements for the organization were primarily due to strong performance in EMG in Q1’25, expanding by 118bps when compared to the previous year’s quarter.

As part of its growth strategy, Ametek measures growth through its Vitality Index, which measures the sales of new products introduced in the trailing 3 years. In Q1’25, this index was up 26%, up from 25% in Q1'24, suggesting continued strength in new product adoption. New products launched in the quarter include the EDAX Octane Elite Ultra EDS/EDX system by GATAN, and the Phantom KT-series high-speed cameras by Vision Research.

The Elite Ultra is an x-ray spectroscopy system that maps and quantifies elements, providing results with speed and accuracy.

The Phantom KT-series cameras integrate custom-designed high-speed sensors from another Ametek business, Forza Silicon, to provide high-quality imaging and performance for industrial testing, motion analysis, or medical research.

Ametek announced the acquisition of Kern Microtechnik in Q1’25 for $103.2mm in cash. Kern is a leading manufacturer of high-precision machining and optical inspection solutions for a variety of markets, including medical, semiconductor, research, and space. By comparison, Kern was a relatively small acquisition with an annual run rate of €50mm in net sales.

Faro Technologies Acquisition

On May 6, 2025, Ametek announced its intent to acquire Faro Technologies for $44/share in cash, valuing Faro at an enterprise value of $920mm, a 44% premium over the preannounced value of FARO shares.

The deal has been approved by each board of directors and is expected to close in H2'25.

Net of synergies and cost reductions, the acquisition may be margin dilutive to Ametek off the bat, given the firm’s high operating margin when compared to Faro. Despite this factor, I suspect the intention of acquiring Faro wasn’t for financial improvement, but rather, the 3D measurement and imaging solutions, as well as its measurement arms, laser scanners and trackers, and software solutions Faro brings to the table. With Ametek’s global presence, Faro may have the capability to grow beyond a $341mm (TTM) in revenue business.

Looking at the combined financials, I’m not anticipating a substantial amount of margin dilution given Faro’s size comparison. I suspect that there may be substantial synergies to be found between the two companies, given the technological breadth of Ametek. This could potentially improve the margins Faro will be bringing into the organization, paired with the appealing growth trajectory for Faro in the coming years.

Tariff Risk

Ametek, like its peers, has exposure to the current tariff environment. Accordingly, management is anticipating the flexibility to pass along additional costs to customers. Given the firm’s geographical footprint, Ametek may have some cushion when navigating international trade, potentially having the ability to offset risks across its global manufacturing footprint. Ametek has roughly 100 manufacturing plants in the U.S. and 50 internationally. Given these factors, management believes that Ametek will be able to localize production and rewrite its supply chains to minimize the impact of tariffs. Despite the potential risks, management reiterated their guidance of a low-single-digit top-line growth rate as well as 3-5% EPS growth.

Ametek may be facing some decline in the Chinese market, with sales down by -10%. Net of China, sales in the Asian market were relatively flat. Management noted that materials sourced in China are primarily for products sold in China. Only 4% of sales in China are imported from the U.S., resulting in a potential $70mm headwind in eQ2’25. Accordingly, a significant proportion of Ametek’s supply chain was shifted out of China in 2018.

In addition to this, management noted in the Q1’25 earnings call that customers are cautious in placing new orders due to the macroeconomic uncertainties, with some delaying project timing. In total, management is expecting a $100mm direct impact from tariffs, which may be mitigated through management’s strategic pricing and supply chain efforts.

Across Ametek’s markets, management is expecting aerospace & defense to grow in the mid-single digits for eFY25, following a strong Q1’25. The power business also realized low-single-digit growth in Q1’25 with a strong pipeline for new business. The power market includes grid modernization and electrification projects, two key growth trends for 2025.

The automation & engineered solutions business faced a low single-digit decline in Q1’25; however, the business experienced sequential growth. New orders improved, particularly in its Paragon Medical business, which realized a 25%+ increase in orders.

Ametek Financial Position

Note: I am not modeling Faro into AMETEK’s financials for eFY25 and beginning modeling Faro into operations beginning in eFY26.

For Q2’25, I’m forecasting Ametek to generate $1,755mm in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $1.72/share. Given the minimal impact of Chinese tariffs and the low 10% universal tariff during trade negotiations, I do not anticipate AME to experience headwinds in sales volumes for the quarter.

Ametek ended Q1’25 with $399mm in cash & equivalent and $1.9b in debt on the balance sheet, for a leverage ratio of 0.69x net debt/aEBITDA. Ametek has $2.5B available under its credit facility, providing significant liquidity going forward.

In Q1’25, Ametek reduced its days inventory to 88 days on hand, down from 96 days in Q1’24. Management noted in the Q1’25 earnings call that some inventory destined for Chinese customers may experience delayed shipments as a result of the tariff policies in place, and that orders were not canceled. This may result in some inventory build in Q2’25 as a result, depending on the ongoing trade war between the U.S. and China.

Faro’s business has certain exposures to tariff risk. Management at Faro mentioned on the Q1’25 earnings call that 1/3rd of Faro’s business comes from software and localized revenue and will not be impacted by tariffs. With all hardware manufactured in Thailand, Faro is relatively well-shielded from higher-impact regions. In total, management is expecting hardware to have a $10mm impact on gross margins, assuming the 36% reciprocal tariff on Thailand remains in place.

Risks Related to Ametek

Bull Case

Ametek has a highly diversified manufacturing footprint that can allow for the firm to mitigate most of the impact from tariffs, with minimal outbound exposure to China. The acquisition of Faro Technologies can deliver significant value over the course of the coming years, given AME’s broad portfolio, potentially allowing for parts and components to be developed in-house, potentially resulting in improved margins for the acquired assets.

Bear Case

The international trade environment is creating a significant amount of uncertainty in the market, potentially resulting in some orders being pushed out to later quarters. This may result in slower top-line growth in the coming quarters, with marginal margin compression. Medical device destocking is still an ongoing headwind for AME; however, new orders are showing signs of improved growth.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

As of Q1’25, AME had $1.25B available under its current share repurchase authorization. In addition to this, Ametek increased its quarterly dividend rate by 11% to $0.31/share in Q1’25.

AME shares currently trade at 18.29x EV/aEBITDA, a slight discount to its midpoint trading premium of 19.40x. From a fundamental perspective, shares are beginning to look appealing for building a position.

Despite the fundamental appeal, AME shares appear to be on a downward trend from a technical perspective, pushing out my timing for purchasing shares.

Given the risks and uncertainties in the market, I am recommending AME with a Hold rating with a price target of $172/share at 16.91x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

FARO shares are currently trading at a -4% discount to the acquisition price. Given that the acquisition is a cash offer, this could provide investors a merger/arbitrage opportunity, assuming the deal goes through.

Looking out beyond the near-term market headwinds, I believe shares should be priced at $188/share at 18.40x eFY26 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model, the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: Amazon Q1: Free Cash Flow Is Concerning
data.description:
AMZN stock: Q1 FCF down 48% YOY

I last analyzed Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) stock on on March 13. That analysis was titled "Amazon Stock Is Oversold, Time To Be Greedy (Technical Analysis, Upgrade)." It argued for a buy rating for the stock with a focus on the technical trading indicators at that time. Quote:

Several technical signals show that Amazon stock is oversold. Its 14-period RSI dipped below 30 in recent trading days (for the first time since last August) and its prices fell below the lower Bollinger Band. Given the robust business fundamentals, I believe the selling is overdone. The current AMZN P/E ratio is an excellent entry point when adjusted for its growth potential.

Since then, there have been a few important developments surrounding AMZN stock both in terms of geopolitics and also company-specific catalysts. In the former category, the tariff disputes that started on April 2 (also known as the "Liberation Day") represent the largest macroeconomic uncertainty. At this point, I think it's difficult to gauge the impact of possible major Chinese tariff effects on third-party sellers and deleveraging of logistics costs, and I will revisit this uncertainty later.

In terms of company-specific catalysts, AMZN released its FQ1 earnings report last week. The results are a bit mixed. On the positive side, the company reported revenue and profit of $155.7 billion and $18.4 billion, both above Wall Street analysis estimates. AWS growth was in line with Wall Street expectations of 17%. But AWS growth decelerated 2 points QOY due to competition (such as Microsoft's (MSFT) Azure acceleration).

The more concerning issue is the large decrease of free cash flow in my model. As you can see from its following earnings report slide, as of Q1 2025, Amazon's FCF has continued its downward trend since Q2 2024. To wit, starting around $50,149 million in Q1 2024, its TTM FCF has decreased to just $25,925 million by Q1 2025. This represents a significant year-over-year decrease of 48%. Next, I will show that such a large decrease of FCF has materially altered the return prospects of the stock, leading to a rating adjustment to Hold under current conditions.

AMZN stock: EPS growth outlook and drivers

Looking ahead, analysts project a steady increase in Amazon's EPS over the coming years. For full fiscal year 2025, the EPS estimate stands at $6.21, with a year-over-year growth of 12.31%. This growth is expected to continue, reaching an estimated $15.83 by FY 2030, representing a compound annual growth rate of 14.3% in the seven fiscal years between now and 2030.

As mentioned above, I see a few uncertainties for such a growth projection. To start, I have new emerging doubts about its AI-related demand and the profitability of its data center business. I think it could be difficult for AWS to sustain the high-teen year-over-year growth rate going ahead with the competition intensification seen in Q1 2025 and also the macroeconomic uncertainties.

But even assuming the above projection materializes, I think the margin of safety is quite thin under current conditions, as detailed next.

AMZN stock is fairly priced

Since I see its FCF as a key concern, it’s fitting to apply the discounted free cash flow to assess its return/risk profile. To wit, I will use a two-stage FCF model with a higher growth rate in the first stage to capture its growth potential in the next few years and the second stage to capture the terminal growth as such potential diminishes. The details of the two-stage FCF model are provided in my earlier article. Only the end results are quoted here to ease reference.

There are a total of three key parameters in the two-stage FCF model: The discount rate, the growth rate in stage 1, and the terminal growth rate. For the discount rate, I relied on the so-called WACC, the weighted average cost of the capital model. The discount rate for AMZN is about 9.47% on average in recent years following this model.

For the growth rate in the first stage, I will just take the 14.7% CAGR projected from consensus to be on the generous side for reasons stated in the forgoing section. For its terminal growth rates, my estimates points to 5.2% with the following method described in an earlier article:

The method involves the return on capital employed (“ROCE”) and the reinvestment rate (“RR”). The ROCE for AMZN has been around 41.6% in the past four years as seen in the chart below. Its RR is about 12.5% on average. With these inputs, AMZN’s perpetual growth rate would be ~5.2% (41.6% ROCE x 12.5% RR = 5.2% terminal growth rate).

With all the three parameters estimated, the table at the end of this section summarizes the results from the discounted FCF model. Note that I plugged in an FWD FCF of $3.65 as my projected FCF for its FY 2025. Under these inputs, the fair value of AMZN stock turned out to be ~$178, slightly below its current market price of $189 as of this writing.

AMZN stock: Other risks and final thoughts

Before closing, a few other risks are worth mentioning but in the upside and downside directions. On the negative side, as aforementioned, the ongoing tariff disputes (especially between China and the U.S.) is a top uncertainty in my model. I could not quantify AMZN’s China exposure based on the updates provided in the earnings report, but I think the impacts could be substantial as low-price “everyday essentials” has been a key growth driver for AMZN and China is the top supplier/manufacture for these essentials. On the positive side, the Prime service continues to post phenomenal results. The number of items received by U.S. Prime members kept growing at rapid rates. The service boasts more than 180 million members domestically and another 20 million overseas, and I expect it to keep providing a stable cash flow to support AMZN’s other initiatives.

To recap, this reassessment of AMZN stock is triggered mainly by two new catalysts since my last writing: The tariff disputes that started on April 2 and also AMZN’s Q1 earnings report. Amazon's Q1 2025 earnings report showed mixed results, and the significant 48% year-over-year decline in its free cash flow is a concern. Even assuming double-digit profit growth of ~14% in the next decade, my fair value estimate for AMZN stock is around ~$178, leaving a thin margin of safety. As such, my verdict is that the upside potential is in balance with downside risks under current conditions, leading to a Hold rating.

data.title: Buckled Up For April's Market Ride
data.description:
In Short

  • Markets experienced heightened volatility in April following “Liberation Day,” with bonds and equities experiencing significant swings. The S&P 500 ultimately returned -0.7% for the month and -4.9% year-to-date.
  • Overall uncertainty has weighed on sentiment and has been a persistent theme in 2025 corporate outlooks, though Q1 earnings season is off to a stronger-than-expected start.
  • In our view, diversification across asset class, region and style remains key in periods of heightened volatility. Investors should continue to take advantage of the market fluctuations to deploy cash in a thoughtful manner.

The Month in Markets

So far this year, ever-changing policy landscape has dominated market behavior, causing a significant spike in volatility. On April 2—coined “Liberation Day”—President Donald Trump revealed his list of reciprocal trade tariffs, sending shockwaves through markets globally. The additional tariffs sparked a significant sell-off in global equities, with the S&P 500 falling over 10% in the subsequent two trading sessions and the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) reaching its highest level since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic.

Some pressure was lifted from equity markets midway through the month when the Trump administration announced a 90-day tariff pause on non-retaliating countries (though proposed tariffs on China reached upwards of 145%), which enforced the 10% “base” on imported goods from most nations. In response, global equity markets barreled higher with the S&P 500 up 9.5% in a single session, its largest gain since October 2008 and third-best single-day performance since 1950. After all the month’s turbulence, the S&P 500 was down only 0.7% in April and 4.9% year-to-date.

Long-dated U.S. Treasuries also experienced a sell-off in April, with the 10-year yield quickly climbing from below 4% to upwards of 4.6%—the biggest single-week jump in almost a quarter century. Although the sell-off has eased (the 10-year was around 4.16% at month-end), it is still cause for concern as U.S. Treasuries are typically viewed as a haven when equities are volatile.

Uncertainty remained a resounding theme as companies began to report Q1 earnings and issue guidance for the remainder of 2025. For example, United Airlines provided two financial outlooks for 2025 given that, in the company’s opinion, it is “impossible to predict” with any degree of confidence how the economy will fare over the rest of the year. On the flipside, earnings at tech companies including Microsoft and Meta showed a renewed emphasis on artificial intelligence as a tailwind for some of the largest stocks in the index. As of the end of the month, over half of S&P 500 companies have reported earnings, with a blended growth rate of +15.5% that is significantly higher than the anticipated +7.2% as of the end of March.

S&P 500 Companies – Earnings Transcript Mentions of ‘Uncertainty’

100 Days With the New Administration

April 29 marked the 100th day of Trump’s second term as U.S. president, which is often used as an occasion to pause and reflect on the incumbent’s tenure thus far. The start to his term has been quite eventful, as Trump issued more executive orders than any modern president within this timeframe, with tariffs representing the cornerstone of his administration’s policy, immediately raising revenues from customs duties even as many argue that the American consumer will bear any longer-term costs.

These immediate actions have had significant near-term impacts on the broader economy. For example, the DXY index, which measures the value of the U.S. dollar against a mix of six other currencies,1 experienced a nearly 10% decline in the relative value of the U.S. dollar since Inauguration Day. Measures of consumer confidence have continued to waver with the Conference Board’s Consumer Confidence Index falling to its lowest level since 2011. As mentioned, equity markets declined as well; in fact, the S&P 500 posted its worst performance in the first 100 days of a presidency since Gerald Ford took office in 1974. Meanwhile, the labor market is holding up well, as shown by April’s jobs report, which at the very least indicates that the U.S. labor market is entering this period of slowing economic growth from a strong position.

Looking ahead, growth remains a concern as reflected by the first preliminary reading of U.S. Q1 GDP, which declined by a 0.3% rate (annualized quarter-over-quarter), falling into contraction territory for the first time since 2022. The stark reversal from the strong growth seen at the end of last year was primarily due to a surge in imports as businesses and consumers raced to get ahead of expected tariffs. However, spending and investment may slow as tariffs drive up prices, and uncertainty keeps businesses on hold.

In addition, if long-term U.S. Treasury rates were to rise above 5% (the direction they were headed early in the month), that would further suppress consumer spending, business confidence and corporate investment—potentially outweighing the effect of tax-cut extensions and deregulation upon which medium-term optimism largely rests. On the other hand, successful negotiations and a clear path toward a rollback of most of the retaliatory tariffs announced on April 2 would likely be necessary to shift sentiment more meaningfully.

All things considered, we think investors could face renewed uncertainty after the current period of calm, and therefore should “stay the course” and look to adhere to long-term strategic asset allocation targets in which diversification across asset class, region and style could provide some buffer to volatility and a level of downside protection. We are also encouraging clients to continue to take advantage of market fluctuations to deploy cash in a thoughtful manner given the opportunities we believe are available in small-cap and non-U.S. equities as well as private markets.

Portfolio Implications

Equities were mixed in April with developed non-U.S. substantially higher in part due to dollar weakness, and U.S. stocks generally lower. We recently upgraded our views on global equities and developed non-U.S. equities due to major fiscal stimulus announced in Europe and ongoing advances in corporate governance in Japan, while maintaining an overweight to small and midcaps as we anticipate better performance against a more predictable policy backdrop and lower rates later in the year. Otherwise, we maintain an “at-target” view across equities. Admittedly, short-term volatility related to tariffs may create pressure on certain sectors and individual stocks that are more heavily exposed to supply chain disruption. However, any larger pullbacks (10% or more) could be an appropriate trigger to add to risk, especially for those holding excess cash. In this more challenging environment, we favor employing active management to select companies with high earnings visibility.

Fixed Income was also mixed with some yield volatility throughout the month. The global aggregate index and shorter-dated bonds moved higher, while domestic longer-dated bonds moved lower. We are more constructive on investment-grade fixed income as yields are close to fair value, with shorter-dated bonds in particular presenting little downside risk, in our opinion. With the spread of bond yields over cash rates likely to widen further, we see opportunities to deploy cash tactically, adding and/or shortening duration based on movement in rates. Multi-sector bond funds may be an appropriate vehicle to consider, given the levers a manager can opportunistically pull across sector, duration profile and region. Overall, our soft-landing outlook and debt sustainability concerns make us cautious on longer-dated bonds, even if policy rates are cut in the latter half of the year.

With a fading liquidity drought and an expected pickup in mergers and acquisitions and other deal activity due to a healthy macro backdrop, we believe significant opportunities still exist within Private Markets, as investors should expect new buyout activity and an unlocking of distributions. That said, liquidity and capital solutions providers will likely remain important to work through the substantial backlog of legacy investments. As a result, we continue to see compelling opportunities across secondaries, mid-life co-investments and capital solutions. We are cautious on core private real estate, but this is offset by what we see as abundant market-dislocation opportunities in the value-add and opportunistic sectors and, particularly, real estate secondaries. Neuberger Berman’s deep relationships and unique position within the private equity ecosystem have translated into record levels of deal flow across our platform.

Index Returns as of April 2025

Apr-25 3M YTD
Equities

S&P 500 Index -0.7% -7.5% -4.9%

ACWI ex US 3.6% 4.8% 9.0%
DM Non-U.S. Equities 4.7% 6.4% 12.0%
EM Equities 1.3% 2.5% 4.4%

Apr-25 3M YTD

Source: Bloomberg, Total returns as of April 30, 2025. S&P 500 Index is represented by S&P 500 Total Return Index. Nasdaq Composite NASDAQ-Composite Total Return Index. Dow Jones is represented by Dow Jones Industrial Average TR. Large Cap is represented by Russell 1000 Total Return Index. Mid Cap is represented by Russell Midcap Index Total Return. Small Cap is represented by Russell 2000 Total Return Index. All Cap is represented by Russell 3000 Total Return Index. Large Cap Growth is represented by Russell 1000 Growth Total Return. Large Cap Value is represented by Russell 1000 Value Index Total Return. Small Cap Growth is represented by Russell 2000 Growth Total Return. Small Cap Value is represented by Russell 2000 Value Total Return. ACWI is represented by MSCI ACWI Net Total Return USD Index. U.S. Aggregate is represented by Bloomberg US Agg Total Return Value Unhedged USD. Munis is represented by Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index Total Return Index Value Unhedged USD. Important Information

1Euro, yen, British pound, Canadian dollar, Swedish krona and Swiss franc.

data.title: Surge In Stock Buybacks On Stretched Valuations A Sign Of Hubris
data.description:
We see increasing evidence of hubris in the equity market as the S&P 500 Index (SPX) continues to defy rapidly deteriorating U.S. macroeconomic fundamentals. Record-shattering stock buyback programs during a time when SPX valuations are at historic highs, while the economy is reeling from the debilitating effects of a trade war, reinforce our view that the bull market is increasingly driven by greed.

According to a recent Bloomberg report, corporate America is buying back shares at a historic pace during a time of great uncertainty on trade and the economy. Announced stock buybacks in the U.S. reached $233.8 billion in April, the second highest monthly record since 1984. Buyback announcements to date are at the highest level ever recorded, surging beyond $600 billion as of May 2.

Corporate giants, including Apple (AAPL), Visa (V), Wells Fargo (WFC), Delta Air Lines (DAL), and 3M (MMM), have all announced plans to repurchase their shares during the current earnings season.

Stock Buybacks Can Be Good, Or Bad

To be clear, stock buybacks are not necessarily a bad thing. Indeed, stock buybacks can be a powerful tool for maximising shareholder value by reinvesting in the company, especially if the stock is undervalued. There are other arguments in favour of or against stock buybacks. From our perspective, however, the most critical factor that maximises shareholder value is when stock buybacks are made at an attractive price level.

The bear market of 2022 is a recent example. The SPX had spiraled into a bear market amid heightened uncertainties over inflation and monetary policy at the time. Although valuations were not exactly cheap, we argued then that there was sufficient evidence to suggest that U.S. macroeconomic fundamentals remained robust and that inflation would eventually be tamed. So, when some of the undervalued companies on our watchlist took the opportunity to aggressively repurchase shares at a deep discount, we upgraded their ratings. Some of these companies include D.R. Horton (DHI) and Blackstone (BX), both of which turned out to be among our best-performing picks in 2023.

The current resurgence in stock buybacks is different. While corporate boardrooms are eager to do more stock buybacks, most valuation measures on the SPX are flashing warning signs of hubris, with current valuation multiples hovering near historic highs.

Of course, stretched valuations do not necessarily imply that a bear market is just around the corner and that any stock buyback would only destroy shareholder value.

During economic booms, it is normal to see earnings growth accelerate and compound over several years, resulting in tremendous value creation for shareholders. Therefore, it is not entirely unreasonable for high-growth companies to be trading at prices that many investors may view as exorbitant or ridiculous.

However, optimism tends to gravitate towards hubris at some point as greedy speculators become willing to pay any price to participate in the opportunity. Typically, this happens when economic fundamentals and data no longer justify or limit a stock's valuation. There is simply no limit to how high a stock can climb so long as speculators are willing to pay for it.

The fact that some companies are expanding their stock buyback programs despite their stock trading near all-time highs should serve as a warning for investors.

Not Just Expensive, But Speculators Are Ignoring Risks

Of course, bullish analysts may argue that high valuation multiples are not a real problem so long as corporate earnings can keep up with the lofty growth expectations. However, given the rapidly deteriorating U.S. macroeconomic picture, which we think is worse than in 2022, we wonder if the current optimism in the equity market is justified.

Most of the arguments in favour of a bullish outlook for the SPX we have encountered lately appear to hinge on three scenarios.

  1. The Trump administration will eventually walk back on tariffs or win favourable trade deals for the U.S.
  2. The Federal Reserve (Fed) will cut rates to save the economy if needed.
  3. The economy is resilient enough to withstand the effects of a trade war.

Although all three bullish scenarios are fairly realistic and likely to happen, we argue that they won't come in time to stave off a recession.

Firstly, the Trump administration is taking on an overly ambitious task of trying to secure complex trade deals with more than 70 nations within just 90 days. Despite the constant hype and promising progress reported by the Trump administration, not a single trade deal has been signed to date. According to a report by CNBC, President Trump is now complaining about everyone constantly checking in on the progress of ongoing trade negotiations. Our view is that many trade deals will eventually be signed, but we are not expecting any big wins for the U.S. from these deals that would have a meaningful impact on the economy.

Secondly, given the inflationary impact that tariffs will inflict on the U.S. economy, a problem that Fed Chairman Jerome Powell has also alluded to in his recent comments, we believe that the Fed will only cut rates meaningfully once it is too late.

Finally, we believe it is no longer a question of whether the U.S. economy will be resilient enough to withstand the effects of a trade war. There is already growing evidence of a recession. In fact, advanced estimates published by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis last week show Q1 2025 U.S. Real GDP contracted at an annual rate of 0.3%.

Although we acknowledge that an abnormal surge in imports may distort the latest Q1 GDP reading due to purchases being brought forward in anticipation of tariffs, there is no denying the fact that the U.S. economy is on the brink of a technical recession (two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth).

Shipping Data Is Looking Bad

Even if one completely ignores the potentially misleading GDP print, one only needs to look at actual shipment data to get a feel of how bad the economy could get.

Below is a chart showing U.S. worldwide exports by ocean carriers plunging after Trump tariffs were imposed in early April. Exports in TEUs (TEU stands for Twenty-Foot Equivalent Unit, a standard unit of measure to quantify cargo capacity) fell from the monthly average of 229K before tariffs to just 57K in April.

Meanwhile, export data showed broad-based declines in TEUs booked across most U.S. ports. All five of the largest U.S. ports witnessed declines in TEUs booked in the five weeks after tariffs took effect.

Given the severity of the data we have presented, the equity market would normally be panicking by now.

Of course, we could be completely wrong about the economy. But based on the data we have seen, we see no reason to believe that the economy is going to be just fine. The stock market can do whatever it wants, but as disciplined investors, we don't want to be caught in the hubris.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Omnicell: Facing New Headwinds
data.description:
Today, we are going to put Omnicell, Inc. (NASDAQ:OMCL), a small cap medical technology solutions provider in the spotlight for the first time. The company just posted its Q1 results on Tuesday. Despite top and bottom line "beats" by the quarterly numbers, the stock fell over 15% in trading on Tuesday. Buy the dip or avoid the "falling knife" scenario? An analysis follows below.

Omnicell, Inc. is headquartered out of Fort Worth, TX. The company provides medication management solutions and adherence tools for healthcare systems and pharmacies both in the United States and globally. The company has two main revenue streams: Products & Services. The services revenue is recurring and consist of SAAS and Technical Services.

The shares trade just north of $25.50 a share and sport an approximate market capitalization of $1.2 billion.

1st Quarter Results:

Omnicell posted its Q1 numbers on May 6th before the opening bell. The company posted non-GAAP profits of 26 cents a share, six cents a share above expectations. Non-GAAP income came in at $12 million, compared to just $1 million or 3 cents a share in the same period a year ago. Non-GAAP EBITDA increased to $24 million versus $11 million. On a GAAP basis, Omnicell had a net loss of $7 million compared to a GAAP net loss of $16 million to in 1Q2024. Non-GAAP gross margins fell 230bps year-over-year to 42.1%. Management blamed the decline on lower product volumes and seasonal expenses.

Revenues rose 9.6% from the same period a year ago to just under $269.7 million, more than $9 million north of the consensus estimate. This was up $24 million from the same period a year ago. Revenues were down $37 million sequentially from the fourth quarter of 2024 due to seasonality. Product Revenue was $145 million, and Services revenue was $125 million.

Despite beating both on top and bottom-line expectations, the stock fell just over 15% Tuesday. Part of this was due to management stating the company will see some tariff impacts. Leadership outlined a $40 million non-GAAP EBITDA impact from tariffs for 2025 and also stated it would mitigate some of these impacts by near shoring and optimizing its supply chain.

Leadership provided the following updated guidance for both Q2 and FY2025. The two big changes from previous guidance issued after fourth quarter results is that FY2025 non-GAAP EBITDA is now predicted to be between $100 million to $145 million range compared to $140 million to $155 million. In addition, non GAAP EPS guidance for the fiscal year has been reduced from $1.65 to $1.85 a share to $1.00 to $1.65 a share. This is a significant drop.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

The analyst firm community is somewhat negative on the company's prospects. Following Q4 results, four analyst firms including in Wells Fargo and JP Morgan maintained Hold ratings, including two with downward price target revisions. Both Piper Sandler ($69 price target) and Benchmark Co. ($62 price target) reissued Buy ratings. Since first quarter results hit the wires yesterday, Craig-Hallum reissued its Buy rating.

Approximately five percent of the float outstanding in the shares is currently held short. The CEO of the company bought nearly $950,000 worth of shares in mid-March. That is the only insider activity in the shares so far in 2025. Omnicell ended the first quarter with $387 million of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet. Total debt was listed at $341 million. The company produced $10 million in free cash flow in Q1.

Conclusion:

Omnicell made $1.71 a share (non-GAAP) on $1.11 billion in revenues in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has profits rising slightly to $1.77 a share as revenues increase barely to $1.13 billion. They project earnings to come in at $1.95 a share in FY2026 on four to five percent revenue growth. Given management's guidance on Tuesday, profit projections for FY2025 if not FY2026 will be taken down significantly.

That leaves the stock trading at 15 times trailing earnings and 1.1 times revenues. The shares do not pay a dividend. While those valuations are a significant discount to the S&P 500 (SP500), they are not compelling. Profits will drop this year, and there is no quarterly dividend payouts. While management took a first swipe at impacts from tariffs, these are initial guesses and there is no certainty on what final tariff policies will eventually be given ongoing trade talks.

Therefore, I am passing on any investment recommendation on Omnicell at this time. Hopefully, by the time second quarter results come out, more clarity will be available around the lay of the land. Both around tariffs and the global economy and their impacts on Omnicell from a longer-term perspective.
